executiveSummary:
Zaporizhzhia National University (ZNU) faces critical eligibility and capability barriers for the ROB4GREEN Open Call #1. While Ukraine is associated with Horizon Europe and Digital Europe Programme (addressing geographic eligibility), ZNU's fundamental mismatch with grant requirements creates insurmountable challenges. The call specifically targets SMEs/Startups as technology integrators partnering with industrial end-users to pilot robotics and AI solutions for circular economy applications (dismantling, remanufacturing, recycling). ZNU, as an academic institution, does not meet the mandatory SME/Startup requirement for the pilot leader role, lacks industrial circular economy experience, has no demonstrated expertise in robotics/physical automation (only HPC/AI software), and crucially has no existing industrial partnerships in manufacturing, waste management, or recycling sectors.

The Laboratory of Parallel and Distributed Computing's 4-person team, while competent in HPC and AI/ML theory, has published primarily in academic journals without demonstrated industrial pilot experience or robotics integration projects. Their current work on AI in logistics addresses national security—completely outside ROB4GREEN's circular economy focus. ZNU's multi-faculty structure and 500+ researchers do not compensate for the absence of robotics engineering, mechatronics, industrial automation, or circular economy research groups. Most critically, the grant structure requires an industrial end-user partner with a real manufacturing/recycling facility, and ZNU has shown no relationships with such entities beyond one construction company.

While academically strong in theoretical computer science, ZNU lacks the industrial orientation, robotics expertise, SME status, and sector partnerships essential for competitiveness. This call funds industry-led innovation pilots, not academic research projects. The 10-month implementation timeline and €300,000 lump-sum structure favor experienced technology providers with existing industrial relationships and proven deployment capabilities—areas where ZNU is fundamentally positioned outside the target beneficiary profile.

breakdown.explanation: Ukraine is associated with Horizon Europe (since 2021) and eligible for participation. This is NOT a barrier despite geographic location.

CRITICAL FAILURE: Grant mandates SME/Startup as pilot leader and technology integrator. ZNU is a university/HEI, explicitly ineligible for this required role. While universities can be optional third partners providing expertise, the lead must be an SME.

Severe mismatch. ROB4GREEN focuses on ROBOTICS (physical automation, manipulators, disassembly systems) + AI for circular economy (dismantling, remanufacturing, recycling). ZNU's expertise is in HPC/parallel computing and AI/ML algorithms—software only. No evidence of robotics engineering, mechatronics, ROS, robotic arm integration, physical automation, or sensor systems. AI capability exists but not applied to robotics or circular economy domains.

No demonstrated experience in circular economy, waste management, remanufacturing, product lifecycle extension, or recycling sectors. Current research (AI in logistics for national security) is in completely different domain. No publications or projects in sustainability, green technology, or circular economy applications.

Major weakness. Grant requires industrial end-user with manufacturing/recycling facility to provide pilot scenario. ZNU lists one construction company (ALD Engineering) but no manufacturers, recyclers, or circular economy operators. Academic partnerships (Durham, Freiberg) don't address the industrial pilot requirement. No evidence of relationships with relevant industrial sectors.

Lab team of 4 researchers (1 PhD, 1 Professor) is small for industrial pilot delivery. Expertise in theoretical HPC/AI, not applied robotics integration. 25 publications primarily academic (Scopus journals), not industry-oriented. No demonstrated pilot implementations, industrial deployments, or technology transfer projects. Track record is research-focused, not innovation/commercialization.

Proposals must integrate 1-2 tools from ROB4GREEN framework (likely including robotics middleware, AI planning systems, digital twin platforms for disassembly). ZNU shows no familiarity with these specific tools, no prior use of industrial robotics frameworks, and no experience integrating third-party robotics/AI platforms into physical systems.

10-month timeline requires rapid deployment in real industrial environment. ZNU lacks robotics hardware infrastructure, industrial pilot facilities, and established processes for industrial technology validation. University procurement and administrative processes typically too slow for aggressive 10-month industrial pilot timelines.

As established university, ZNU likely meets basic requirements (VAT, PIC number, financial stability, legal standing). However, university financial systems may struggle with lump-sum payment model and sprint-based milestone delivery more suited to agile SMEs.

strengths.description: Ukraine's association with Horizon Europe and Digital Europe Programme removes any geographic barriers. ZNU is fully eligible to participate based on location.
Laboratory team has competence in AI/ML algorithms, parallel computing, and optimization—foundational skills that could support AI components of robotics solutions.

18 faculties including Engineering Institute, Physics, Mathematics, and Biology provide potential for cross-disciplinary collaboration if robotics/circular economy expertise exists elsewhere in university.
As accredited national university with 500+ researchers, ZNU has administrative infrastructure for grant management, financial compliance, and project administration.

weaknesses.area: Entity Type Ineligibility for Lead Role
weaknesses.description: Grant explicitly requires SME/Startup as pilot leader and technology integrator. Universities can only participate as optional third partner (expertise provider). ZNU cannot lead a consortium for this call.
weaknesses.mitigation: Could only participate as third partner if recruited by an eligible SME leading the project. Would need to find SME partner in robotics/circular economy willing to lead, which shifts ZNU to supporting role with limited budget allocation.

weaknesses.description: No demonstrated capability in physical robotics, robotic manipulation, mechatronics, industrial automation, or robot integration. Software AI capability exists but robotics requires hardware/software co-design, ROS, sensor fusion, motion planning—none evidenced.
weaknesses.mitigation: Would require recruiting robotics faculty or partnering with Engineering Institute departments. However, no evidence such expertise exists within ZNU. External robotics partner essential but undermines ZNU's value proposition.

weaknesses.description: Zero track record in dismantling, remanufacturing, recycling, product lifecycle management, or waste valorization. Current projects in logistics/national security are completely different application domains.
weaknesses.mitigation: Cannot be quickly addressed. Domain expertise requires years of applied research. Would need to partner with circular economy experts, further diluting ZNU's role and contribution.

weaknesses.description: Grant mandates industrial partner with manufacturing/recycling facility to host pilot. ZNU has no demonstrated relationships with manufacturers, recyclers, or circular economy operators who could provide realistic pilot scenarios.
weaknesses.mitigation: Must identify and secure commitment from industrial end-user in next 2 months before deadline. Cold outreach unlikely to succeed. No warm introductions or existing relationships evident. Industrial partners seek experienced technology providers, not academic newcomers.

weaknesses.area: Small Team Size for Industrial Pilot
weaknesses.description: Laboratory team of 4 researchers insufficient for intensive 10-month industrial deployment requiring on-site integration, troubleshooting, iteration, and validation in operational environment.
weaknesses.mitigation: Would need to expand team significantly, hire industrial engineers, or collaborate with Engineering Institute. Budget constraints (max €200k per entity) limit hiring capacity. 10-month timeline too short for team building.

weaknesses.description: Track record shows academic publication focus (Scopus journals, theoretical research) rather than industrial pilot implementations, technology transfer, or commercialization activities required for this innovation-focused call.
weaknesses.mitigation: Requires fundamental shift in research approach. Academic institutions can succeed in industrial pilots but need established industrial collaboration mechanisms, technology transfer offices, and commercialization experience—not evident at ZNU.

weaknesses.description: HPC laboratory has computing infrastructure but no evidence of robotics testbeds, robotic arms, automated assembly/disassembly systems, or physical prototyping facilities needed to develop and validate solutions before industrial deployment.
weaknesses.mitigation: Could access industrial partner's equipment, but increases dependency and reduces ZNU's ability to iterate independently. Purchasing robotics equipment would consume significant budget portion, leaving less for actual pilot work.

weaknesses.description: No indication of awareness or prior use of ROB4GREEN tools. Proposals must integrate specific framework components, requiring learning curve during already compressed 10-month timeline.
weaknesses.mitigation: Can study ROB4GREEN documentation during proposal preparation, but lack of hands-on experience reduces proposal credibility and increases technical risk. Evaluators will favor applicants with demonstrated framework knowledge.

gaps.description: No robotics engineers, mechatronics specialists, industrial automation experts, or circular economy researchers identified in Laboratory or broader university structure.
gaps.recommendation: Conduct internal audit of Engineering Institute and technical faculties to identify any hidden robotics/automation capabilities. If none exist, must recruit external robotics partner organization, which fundamentally changes project structure and ZNU's role.

gaps.description: Zero demonstrated industrial pilot implementations, technology deployments in operational environments, or validation of research prototypes in real-world settings.
gaps.recommendation: This gap cannot be filled before April 2026 deadline. Consider targeting future calls after building track record through smaller national/bilateral industrial collaboration projects. ROB4GREEN Open Call #2 may be more realistic target after gaining experience.

gaps.description: Absence of robotics hardware labs, automated disassembly testbeds, recycling process simulation facilities, or physical infrastructure for circular economy technology development.
gaps.recommendation: Partner with organization possessing these resources or propose pilot entirely at industrial partner's facility. However, this reduces ZNU to software/algorithm provider only, weakening competitive position against specialized robotics SMEs.

gaps.description: No experience with industrial robotics middleware (ROS, ROS2), robot programming frameworks, industrial protocols (OPC-UA, etc.), or integration with manufacturing execution systems required for industrial deployments.
gaps.recommendation: Team members could complete online training in ROS and industrial robotics, but shallow knowledge won't compensate for lack of hands-on integration experience. Better to acknowledge gap and partner with experienced robotics integrator.

gaps.description: University administrative processes (procurement, contracting, financial reporting) typically slower and more rigid than agile industrial pilot requirements with sprint-based milestones and rapid iteration needs.
gaps.recommendation: Establish dedicated project management structure with fast-track approval processes. However, institutional constraints may remain. SME partners inherently better suited to agile 10-month pilot timelines.

risks.description: Fundamental entity type mismatch—university cannot serve as required SME/Startup pilot leader. Could only participate as optional third partner, requiring complete restructuring of participation approach.
risks.mitigation: Abandon lead applicant role. Instead, identify robotics/circular economy SME willing to lead and invite ZNU as expertise partner. Dramatically reduces budget share and project control. May not be worth effort given limited contribution potential.

risks.description: Even if eligibility solved through partnership structure, lack of robotics and circular economy expertise creates high probability of unsuccessful pilot implementation, inability to integrate ROB4GREEN tools effectively, or failure to meet technical milestones.
risks.mitigation: Only viable if ZNU recruits multiple specialized partners covering all gaps (robotics SME for hardware/integration, circular economy industrial end-user, possibly additional domain experts). At that point, ZNU's value-add becomes marginal—essentially just providing AI algorithms that robotics SMEs likely already possess.

risks.description: Likely competitors include specialized robotics SMEs with track records in industrial automation, AI companies focused on manufacturing/recycling applications, and established circular economy technology providers—all with demonstrated pilot experience and industrial networks. ZNU's academic profile and lack of relevant track record make it highly uncompetitive.
risks.mitigation: Cannot meaningfully compete in this call without fundamental repositioning. Better strategy: use this as learning opportunity to understand industrial robotics/circular economy landscape, build relationships for future opportunities, and focus on more academia-appropriate funding programs.

risks.description: Only 2 months until April 8 deadline. Identifying, engaging, and securing commitments from both qualified SME lead (if ZNU participates as third partner) AND industrial end-user with suitable pilot scenario is extremely challenging timeframe, especially without existing relationships.
risks.mitigation: Immediate outreach to European robotics associations, circular economy networks, and recycling industry groups. However, cold networking rarely yields committed partnerships in 2-month window. Industrial partners need confidence in technology provider's capabilities—hard to establish quickly.

risks.description: 10-month timeline with sprint-based milestones and 50% payment tied to completion is aggressive for industrial pilot. University administrative procedures, procurement delays, and inexperience with rapid industrial deployment cycles create high risk of timeline slippage and milestone failures.
risks.mitigation: Pre-negotiate fast-track university procedures, establish dedicated project team with industrial secondments or full-time industrial liaisons, and build significant buffer into timeline. However, structural constraints of academic institutions remain inherent limitation.

risks.description: €300k maximum for 2-3 partners over 10 months must cover technology development, ROB4GREEN tool integration, industrial pilot deployment, travel, equipment, and personnel. University salary scales and overhead may consume disproportionate share, leaving insufficient funds for robotics hardware, industrial site activities, and iterations.
risks.mitigation: Careful budget allocation with majority to industrial partners for implementation. However, this further reduces ZNU's role and budget share. Universities typically less cost-effective than SMEs for industrial pilot work due to overhead structures.

risks.description: Submitting weak proposal outside organization's competency area, or worse, winning but failing to deliver successful pilot, could damage ZNU's reputation with EU funding bodies and limit future opportunities in areas where university is actually competitive.
risks.mitigation: Honest self-assessment should lead to decision NOT to pursue this call. Preserve reputation capital for better-matched opportunities. If proceeding despite risks, ensure exceptionally strong partners who can compensate for ZNU's limitations and guarantee successful delivery.

requiredActions.action: DECISION POINT: Determine if pursuing this call is strategic use of resources given fundamental mismatches. Consider if effort better spent on more suitable funding opportunities aligned with academic strengths (Horizon Europe research actions, education/capacity building grants, bilateral research cooperation).

If proceeding: Identify potential SME lead partner in robotics/circular economy willing to include ZNU as third partner. Search European robotics associations (euRobotics), circular economy networks (Ellen MacArthur Foundation partners), EIT Manufacturing/EIT Climate-KIC member organizations.

If proceeding: Secure industrial end-user partner with manufacturing/recycling facility and specific circular economy challenge. Target Ukrainian or Eastern European manufacturers (shorter cultural/language distance), recycling operations, or remanufacturing facilities. Need signed letter of intent.

Conduct internal capability audit: Survey Engineering Institute and technical faculties for ANY robotics, automation, mechatronics, or circular economy expertise not represented in HPC Laboratory. If exists, recruit to proposal team immediately.

Study ROB4GREEN framework tools in detail. Review project website, technical documentation, pilot scenarios, and webinars. Identify which tools ZNU could realistically integrate and contribute to (likely AI/data analytics components rather than robotics hardware).

Register for PIC number on EU Funding & Tenders Portal if not already registered. Verify VAT registration details. Prepare financial capacity documentation (university financial statements, declaration of honor).

If partnerships secured: Define clear value proposition for ZNU's role. Focus on AI/ML algorithm development for circular economy optimization, data analytics for process improvement, or HPC-based simulation—areas where university actually has expertise. Avoid overpromising on robotics integration.

Contact ROB4GREEN consortium via oc@rob4green-project.eu with specific questions about university participation as third partner, examples of academic institutions in similar roles, and clarification on entity type requirements.
Review proposal templates (Annexes 3, 4, 5, 6) and familiarize team with F6S platform submission process. Assign administrative staff to handle submission logistics.

Document lessons learned from this evaluation. Use insights to identify more appropriate future funding opportunities where ZNU's academic strengths in HPC/AI research, education, and theoretical innovation are competitive advantages rather than limitations.

strategicRecommendations: PRIMARY RECOMMENDATION: Do not pursue ROB4GREEN Open Call #1 as lead applicant. The fundamental mismatch between grant requirements (SME-led industrial pilot in robotics/circular economy) and ZNU's capabilities (academic HPC/AI research) makes success extremely unlikely and resource investment inefficient.

ALTERNATIVE STRATEGY: If ZNU has strategic reasons to engage with robotics/circular economy domain (e.g., building new research direction), consider this a learning/networking opportunity rather than competitive funding pursuit. Use proposal development process to map ecosystem, identify potential partners, and understand industrial requirements—but with realistic expectation of rejection.

PARTNERSHIP APPROACH: Only proceed if exceptional SME partner approaches ZNU with specific need for HPC/AI algorithms that ZNU uniquely provides. In this scenario, participate as junior third partner (€50-75k budget share) providing clearly scoped algorithmic support, NOT leading technical integration or pilot implementation.

GEOGRAPHIC ADVANTAGE: Ukraine's Horizon Europe association is strategic asset—but leverage it for appropriate programs. Look for Horizon Europe MSCA doctoral/postdoctoral networks, research infrastructure access (EuroHPC), Twinning/Teaming actions, or capacity-building programs where academic institutions lead. Don't waste geographic eligibility on mismatched innovation calls.

BUILD TRACK RECORD: Before pursuing industrial pilot funding, establish credibility through smaller steps: (1) Join existing robotics research consortia as AI/data partner, (2) Collaborate with Ukrainian industrial partners on proof-of-concept projects, (3) Publish in robotics/automation venues showing applied work, (4) Attend circular economy industry events to build networks. This positions ZNU for ROB4GREEN Open Call #2 or similar future opportunities.

FOCUS ON ACADEMIC STRENGTHS: ZNU's HPC Laboratory would be highly competitive for: Horizon Europe research grants in HPC/AI (HORIZON-CL4 Digital/Emerging topics), EuroHPC access programs (JU-PCP), Digital Europe capacity-building (DIGITAL-SKILLS), education/training initiatives (Erasmus+), and bilateral research cooperation (bilateral S&T agreements). These align with academic mission and existing capabilities.

ENGINEERING INSTITUTE EXPLORATION: ZNU's Engineering Institute (formerly Zaporizhzhia State Engineering Academy) may have mechanical engineering, industrial automation, or manufacturing expertise separate from HPC Laboratory. If so, this could be foundation for future robotics capability development. Conduct thorough internal assessment before dismissing all possibilities.

CIRCULAR ECONOMY EDUCATION ANGLE: If determined to engage with circular economy, consider education-focused opportunities instead of industrial pilots. ZNU could develop circular economy curriculum, training programs for workforce upskilling, or educational materials—leveraging university's teaching mission rather than competing in industrial technology deployment.

WARTIME CONTEXT CONSIDERATION: Ukraine's ongoing security situation may actually HELP position certain proposals around resilience, sustainability, or reconstruction applications. If pursuing robotics/circular economy work, frame within Ukraine's post-war reconstruction needs, sustainable rebuilding priorities, or resilience through local manufacturing/recycling capacity.

CONSORTIA SCANNING: Monitor EIT Manufacturing, EIT Climate-KIC, and other European innovation communities for open calls better suited to academic partners. These often have specific roles for universities in innovation ecosystems (education, research, validation) without requiring SME status or industrial pilot leadership.

REALISTIC SELF-ASSESSMENT: ZNU's HPC/AI capabilities are valuable—but not for THIS call. Successful grant strategy requires matching organizational strengths to funder priorities. ROB4GREEN targets established robotics SMEs with circular economy track records. ZNU is emerging academic HPC group building AI research portfolio. These profiles don't align. Acknowledge this honestly and redirect energy to winnable opportunities.

LONG-TERM POSITIONING: If robotics/circular economy represents genuine strategic direction for ZNU, invest in 2-3 year capability building: Hire robotics faculty, establish automation lab, partner with Ukrainian manufacturing industry, publish applied work, and build demonstrable track record. THEN pursue industrial pilot funding from position of strength rather than current position of capability gap.
